16: A type: 'Then the LORD raised up judges, who saved them out of the hand of those who plundered them.' Each judge is a partial, flawed type of the divine deliverer: they are raised up by YHWH, empowered by his Spirit, deliver Israel from oppression, provide rest during their tenure, and then die — leaving Israel without a permanent deliverer. The entire judge-cycle is the OT's lived demonstration that the people need a permanent deliverer, a judge who will not die, a king who will reign forever. Christ fulfills all three mediatorial roles of the judges (prophet, priest, king) permanently and perfectly: he is raised up by the Father, anointed without measure by the Spirit, delivers his people from the oppressor (sin and death), and reigns forever — so that the Deuteronomic cycle is permanently broken in him.
1: A type: the Angel of the LORD ascending from Gilgal to Bochim is YHWH himself in theophanic form (cf. Judges 6:11-24; Judges 13:2-22 where the Angel is recognized as God). He reaffirms the covenant — “I brought you up from Egypt and I brought you into the land” — and announces the consequences of disobedience. The Angel of the LORD who mediates covenant history is the same divine Person who appears in Christ: John 1:18 (“the only Son, who is at the Father’s side, has made him known”).
2: A theme: the covenant requirements — no covenant with the inhabitants, break down their altars — were not kept. The total demand of the covenant (exclusive allegiance, destruction of competing worship) mirrors the NT demand of total consecration to Christ: “Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers” (2 Corinthians 6:14); “you cannot serve God and money” (Matthew 6:24). The impossibility of perfect covenant-keeping by human effort points to the need for a covenant partner who will keep it on behalf of his people.
3: A theme: the nations become “thorns in your sides” and “their gods will be a snare to you.” The consequence of incomplete obedience is ongoing trouble from what was not fully dealt with. This pattern of unresolved sin becoming a persistent snare is addressed in Christ’s complete atonement: he not only forgives but destroys the power of sin (1 John 3:8: “The Son of God appeared to destroy the works of the devil”).
4: A revelation of God: Israel weeps (wayyiśśəʾû qôlām wayyiḇkû) when confronted with their covenant failure. The divine word, when faithfully delivered, produces genuine mourning over sin — the godly grief that leads to repentance (2 Corinthians 7:10). Christ’s proclamation similarly produces grief that leads to life: the Beatitude “blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted” (Matthew 5:4) names this as a mark of kingdom citizens.
5: A theme: the place is named Bochim (“weepers”) and they sacrifice to YHWH there. The naming of a place by the spiritual experience that occurred there is an OT pattern of memorial — these places function as reminders of divine encounter and covenant reckoning. The sacrifices at Bochim anticipate the sacrifice of Christ, in whom every divine-human reconciliation finds its ground.
6: A theme: Joshua dismisses the people to their inheritances. The covenant community scattered to receive what has been allotted to them mirrors the NT vision of the church scattered into the world to occupy their calling while waiting for the full inheritance. Each tribe’s inheritance is a partial anticipation of the “inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven” (1 Peter 1:4).
7: A theme: the people served YHWH all the days of Joshua and the elders who outlived him — those who had seen YHWH’s work. Faithfulness is easier when living witnesses of divine action are present. The NT community is called to continue in faith beyond the eyewitness generation: “blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed” (John 20:29). The Spirit’s role in maintaining faith across generations (replacing the eyewitness function) is the guarantee of this continuity.
8: A theme: Joshua “the servant of the LORD” (ʿeḇed YHWH) dies at 110 — the same age as Joseph (Genesis 50:26). The ʿeḇed YHWH title connects Joshua to the Servant Songs of Isaiah; Joshua is a faithful servant who accomplishes his mission and dies. Isaiah’s Servant (Isa 53) also dies — but in his death accomplishes the ultimate redemptive mission, and unlike Joshua, he does not leave a void of leadership.
9: A theme: Joshua is buried at Timnath-heres (or Timnath-serah; “portion of the sun”) in the hill country of Ephraim. Every deliverer is eventually buried, and the community must continue without them. Only Christ — buried and then raised — provides a continuing leadership that does not depend on successors (Hebrews 7:25: “he always lives to make intercession for them”).
10: A shadow: “There arose another generation after them who did not know the LORD or the work that he had done for Israel.” This spiritual amnesia is the root of the judge cycle. The new covenant addresses this structural vulnerability: “I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts” (Jeremiah 31:33); the Spirit is given so that each believer has direct knowledge of God, not merely inherited tradition (1 John 2:27).
11: A theme: Israel abandons YHWH and serves the Baals. The shift from the living God to fertility-deity worship is the archetypal apostasy; “doing what was evil in the sight of the LORD” is the Deuteronomistic judgment formula. The NT frames idolatry similarly: “flee from idolatry” (1 Corinthians 10:14); “guard yourselves from idols” (1 John 5:21). Idolatry’s deepest form is whatever displaces Christ as the center of one’s devotion.
12: A theme: they abandoned YHWH “who had brought them out of the land of Egypt” — the Exodus redemption is the anchor of covenant identity, and to abandon YHWH is to abandon one’s origin and identity. Paul uses the same logic for NT believers: abandoning Christ is to forget that you were “bought with a price” (1 Corinthians 6:20), that you were redeemed from slavery to sin.
13: A theme: “they abandoned the LORD and served Baal and the Ashtoreths.” The Ashtoreths (fertility goddesses) represent the reduction of divine worship to the management of natural cycles and human prosperity. The prophets repeatedly describe this as spiritual adultery (Hosea 1-3; Ezek 16). Christ’s claim to be the resurrection and the life (John 11:25) — not merely the manager of fertility and fortune — is the antidote to this reduction.
14: A revelation of God: YHWH’s anger is not passive or impotent — he sells them into the hand of plunderers and gives them over to their enemies. Divine discipline is an expression of covenant love (Hebrews 12:6: “the Lord disciplines the one he loves”). The same God who delivers also permits difficulty when his people persist in covenant unfaithfulness; both actions serve the purpose of restoring the relationship.
15: A revelation of God: “the hand of the LORD was against them for harm, as the LORD had warned, and they were in terrible distress.” The covenant curses of Deut 28 are activated. The wrath of God against covenant unfaithfulness is real — and it is precisely this wrath that Christ absorbs on the cross (Romans 3:25; Galatians 3:13), so that those who are in him are no longer under the covenant curse.
17: A shadow: even after judges were raised up, the people “did not listen to their judges, for they whored after other gods.” Every human deliverer is ultimately ineffective at producing lasting covenant faithfulness. The judge-cycle’s escalating corruption — “they were more corrupt than their fathers” (v19) — demonstrates that external deliverance cannot change the heart. The new covenant’s promise of inner transformation (Ezekiel 36:26-27) is the only solution to the cycle that Judges documents.
18: A type: “whenever the LORD raised up judges for them, the LORD was with the judge, and he saved them from the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge.” This verse distills the judge-type: raised up by YHWH, accompanied by divine presence, delivering from enemies, providing rest during their tenure. Every element maps onto Christ — raised up by the Father (Acts 2:32), the divine presence incarnate (Matthew 1:23), the deliverer from all enemies (Colossians 2:15), the one in whom the Sabbath-rest of God is found (Matthew 11:28-30).
19: A shadow: “whenever the judge died, they turned back and were more corrupt than their fathers.” The death of each judge undoes the deliverance; the cycle restarts and deepens in depravity. This structural problem — the deliverer’s death reverses his work — is resolved only in Christ whose death does not undo but accomplishes his saving work, and whose resurrection means his deliverance cannot be reversed (Romans 6:9: “Christ, being raised from the dead, will never die again”).
20: A revelation of God: YHWH’s anger is expressed in covenantal terms: “this people has transgressed my covenant that I commanded their fathers.” The violation is relational — covenant, not merely legal — and YHWH’s response is relational discipline. The new covenant established in Christ’s blood (Luke 22:20) does not abolish the covenantal structure but fulfills it: Christ perfectly keeps the covenant on behalf of his people (2 Corinthians 1:20).
21: A theme: YHWH will no longer drive out the remaining nations — their presence will serve as a test. The nations left in the land become the means of sanctification: Israel must choose faithfulness under pressure. The NT parallel is the continuing presence of temptation and opposition in the believer’s life, which God permits not because he is absent but because it produces mature faith (James 1:2-4; Romans 5:3-4).
22: A theme: the nations remain “in order to test Israel by them, whether they will take care to walk in the way of the LORD as their fathers did.” Testing as a divine pedagogical instrument runs throughout Scripture: Abraham’s testing (Gen 22), Israel in the wilderness (Deuteronomy 8:2), Jesus’ temptation in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1). Christ’s perfect obedience through testing establishes the standard that the new covenant makes available to believers through the Spirit.
23: A theme: YHWH “left those nations, not driving them out quickly.” The divine patience in not immediately resolving every opposition — giving time for covenant engagement and testing — mirrors the eschatological patience of the NT: “the Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish” (2 Peter 3:9). The incompleteness of the present age is purposeful.
